1: The great courtroom scene opens: coastlands summoned to silence, nations called to renew their strength, and then to come near to speak. The silence first -- no speech from the defendant until the court is formally assembled. The forensic framework (lawsuit, case, arguments) will run through Isaiah 43. The MKT's let them come near and then speak captures the procedural sequence: assemble, then present your case. Together (yachdav) they are to draw near for judgment.
2: Melek (king, disp 2) is flagged -- he delivers nations before him and treads down kings. The conqueror from the east is Cyrus of Persia, the LORD's anointed instrument (Isaiah 44:28; Isaiah 45:1), who came from the east and defeated kings with remarkable speed. The MKT renders the kings as the objects of Cyrus's conquest. The key translation question is whether this is primarily historical (Cyrus) or typological (the ultimate Conqueror who defeats death and its principalities) -- the MKT allows both.
3: Shalom (peace, disp 2) is flagged -- the MKT renders traveling paths his feet have never trodden before. The Hebrew shalom here means safely/unharmed rather than covenantal peace -- be-shalom means in safety, in wholeness, without being pursued successfully. The conqueror passes through without hindrance, as if the resistance simply melts away. The MKT's safely is the correct sense in this military-transit context.
4: YHWH is flagged -- the self-revelation formula: I, the LORD -- I was first, and I am with the last. The Hebrew ani rishon ve-et-acharonim ani-hu is the foundational divine I-am formula of Second Isaiah, claiming to be first and last simultaneously. Revelation 1:17 and 22:13 apply this formula to the risen Christ: I am the first and the last. The MKT's rendering is close to the Hebrew word order, preserving the double I (the subject and the predicate) that creates the formula's weight.
5: The coastlands saw the conqueror's advance and were afraid -- the ends of the earth trembled. The reaction of fear at divine action is the expected theophanic response. But their fear drives them not to YHWH but to their own mutual encouragement (v6) and idol-making (v7) -- the same fear that should produce repentance instead produces religious panic that generates more idolatry.
6: The nations encourage each other -- take courage! The mutual reinforcement of the idol-manufacturing culture: craftsman to goldsmith, craftsman to craftsman. The social dimension of idolatry -- it is communally maintained and mutually reinforced -- is Isaiah's consistent observation. No one idol-maker works alone; the system is collaborative. The encouragement (chazaq: be strong!) is the same word used for YHWH's strengthening of Israel (v10); both the idolater and the LORD use it, to opposite ends.
7: The craftsman encourages the goldsmith; the one who smooths with the hammer encourages the one striking the anvil. The manufacturing process is described step by step: the technological competence is real -- the nails are good, the image will not fall -- but the product is nothing. The competent manufacture of nothing is Isaiah's ironic point. The MKT's joins the soldering captures the final stage of metalwork idol-production.
8: But you, Israel, my servant, Jacob, whom I have chosen, offspring of Abraham my friend. The contrast after vv6-7 is deliberate: while the nations encourage each other to make nothing, Israel is addressed in terms of covenantal relationship. Three titles: my servant, my chosen one, offspring of Abraham my friend. The word friend (ohavi: the one I love) for Abraham is unique here in the OT -- 2 Chronicles 20:7 and James 2:23 use it. The covenant history grounds the present promise.
9: YHWH's election of Israel from the ends of the earth and calling from the farthest regions. The you are my servant; I have chosen you and not rejected you language is the anti-rejection formula: the fear that YHWH has cast off his people is explicitly countered. The MKT preserves the geographic scope of the calling: from the ends of the earth -- not from somewhere nearby but from the farthest possible distance, showing the reach of divine calling.
10: Elohim is flagged -- do not be dismayed, for I am your God. The four divine actions: I will strengthen you (amatzekha), I will help you (azartikha), I will uphold you (tamakhti) with my righteous right hand. The pattern is past-guarantee-for-future: the same God who called you from the ends of the earth will continue to strengthen, help, and uphold. The right hand of righteousness is the hand that acts in covenant faithfulness.
11: All who are enraged against Israel will be put to shame and disgraced; those who oppose will come to nothing. The reversal formula: the shaming of the shamers, the nothing-ing of those who tried to destroy. The MKT's will come to nothing renders ke'efes (like nothing/zero): not just defeated but reduced to non-being, using the same word for the nothingness of idols (v24, 29).
12: You will seek your adversaries and not find them -- the enemies will simply be absent, not just defeated. The MKT's will be as nothing at all (ke'ayin) is the nothing-formula: total absence rather than merely military defeat. The enemies who once dominated Israel will not be findable -- not because they fled but because they have ceased to exist as a threat.
13: Elohim and YHWH are flagged -- For I, the LORD your God, hold your right hand; I am the one who says to you, Do not fear; I will help you. The divine hand holding the human right hand is an intimate protection-image: the same posture as a parent holding a child's hand across a dangerous road. The MKT's hold your right hand is literal; the personal address (I am the one who says to you) makes the divine help personal and direct.
14: YHWH is flagged -- Do not be afraid, O Jacob, you worm; you few men of Israel! The address you worm (tola'at) is remarkable: Israel is not described as a great nation but as a worm, a creature of weakness and smallness. This is not contemptuous but protective -- the divine help is specifically for the weak. The MKT's you worm preserves the shock of the address. The Redeemer (go'el: kinsman-redeemer) is the LORD -- Psalms 22:6 uses the same word for the Messiah's self-description in abandonment.
15: The worm-to-threshing-sledge transformation: I will make you a new, sharp threshing sledge with many cutting edges. The transformation is from weakness to instrument -- but the instrument serves YHWH's purposes, not Israel's self-aggrandizement. The mountains threshed and the hills reduced to chaff: the cosmic scope of the renewed Israel's function as YHWH's instrument in the world. The MKT's new, sharp threshing sledge with many cutting edges is literal and vivid.
16: YHWH is flagged; ruach (spirit, disp 4) is flagged -- the wind will carry them away and the gale will scatter them; you will rejoice in the LORD. The ruach here is the natural wind/gale that disperses the threshed chaff -- not the divine Spirit but atmospheric wind. The MKT's wind and gale are correct for this physical context. Then the promised response: you will rejoice in the LORD, glory in the Holy One of Israel -- the covenant joy that follows the divine act.
17: Elohim and YHWH are flagged -- I, the LORD their God, will answer them; I, the God of Israel, will not forsake them. The double divine self-identification (LORD their God / God of Israel) for answering the poor and needy seeking water. The divine answer to thirst is the transformation of the landscape (vv18-19): rivers on bare heights, fountains in valleys, wilderness into pools. The material provision is real, not merely spiritual metaphor.
18: Rivers on bare heights, fountains in the middle of valleys, wilderness into pools, dry land into springs of water -- the comprehensive transformation of the arid landscape into a well-watered land. The MKT preserves the environmental specificity: the heights (normally dry), the valleys (normally prone to flooding but here fountains), the wilderness (arid zone), the dry land. Each inhospitable feature becomes hospitable by divine action.
19: The seven trees planted in the wilderness and desert: cedar, acacia, myrtle, olive, cypress, plane, and pine. The seven-tree list is a kind of paradise-restoration catalogue -- the most valuable timber and shade trees of the ancient Near East, now planted in the places of desolation. The MKT's precision in tree identification follows the Hebrew list; some identification is uncertain but the cumulative point is clear: where nothing grew, everything grows.
20: YHWH is flagged; yada (know, disp 2) is flagged -- so that they may see and know, consider and understand together, that the hand of the LORD has done this. The four verbs of apprehension (see, know, consider, understand) all point toward the same recognition: this transformation is the LORD's doing, not natural or human process. The MKT's together (yachdav) preserves the communal dimension of this recognition -- it is not private but shared.
21: YHWH and melek (king, disp 2) are flagged -- the courtroom resumes: Present your case, says the LORD; bring forward your strongest arguments, says the King of Jacob. The idols are called to give evidence. The divine title King of Jacob applies the royal title to the covenantal relationship: YHWH is not merely a universal sovereign but specifically Israel's King. The MKT's King of Jacob is literal, consistent with the election-language of vv8-9.
22: Yada (know, disp 2) is flagged -- the challenge to the idols: tell us what the former things were, what they mean, so that we may know their outcome. The idol is challenged to prove its knowledge of past events and future events -- the two realms where divine foreknowledge would demonstrate genuine divinity. The MKT's so we can consider them renders the reflective purpose: the knowledge is sought for evaluation, not mere curiosity.
23: Elohim is flagged; yada is flagged -- Tell us what is to come in the future, so that we may know you are gods. The test of divinity: predictive knowledge of the future. The MKT renders yeda (know) as know you are gods -- the knowing is recognition of divine status. The invitation to do good or do evil -- do something is the desperation of an unjust judge allowing anything: if the idols cannot predict, can they at least act? The answer is no.
24: The verdict: you are nothing and your work amounts to nothing; whoever chooses you is an abomination. The nothing-verdict (efes: zero, nothingness) applied to the idols and their makers' work. Choosing idols is an abomination (to'evah) -- the same word used for the Canaanite practices forbidden in Deuteronomy. The courtroom verdict against idolatry is total: not just ineffective but morally offensive to YHWH.
25: I have stirred up one from the north and one from the east -- Cyrus of Persia (from the east/northeast) who will come calling on YHWH's name. The trampling of rulers as on mortar, like a potter treading clay, is the image of the victorious king reducing his enemies to raw material. The MKT preserves the potter-clay image that will be developed in 45:9 and 64:8 for divine sovereignty over creation.
26: Yada (know, disp 2) is flagged -- Who declared this from the beginning, so we could know? The repeated rhetorical question exposes the silence of the idols: no one among them declared it, no one foretold it, no one heard their words. The knowledge-through-prediction test has been failed by all the idols and their advocates. The MKT's so we could know preserves the purpose clause that makes the prediction a knowledge-event.
27: YHWH's self-claim: I was the first to declare to Zion, look -- here they are! The divine announcement-priority -- the LORD was first to announce the good news to Jerusalem. The messenger of good news (mevasser) is the word that becomes euangelion in Greek -- the gospel herald. This verse anticipates the great herald-announcement of 52:7 and the NT's appropriation of Isaianic herald-language for the gospel proclamation.
28: I looked and there was no one -- among all the idols and nations, no counselor, no one who could answer. The silence of the idols in the courtroom is complete: not even a single spokesman. The MKT's when I asked them, not one could answer captures the total absence of divine speech from the idols. The contrast with the LORD who speaks throughout this courtroom scene is the structural argument.
29: Ruach (spirit/wind, disp 4) is flagged -- their cast images are just wind and futility. Here ruach means wind/emptiness -- not the divine Spirit but the hollow breath of nothing. The MKT's wind is correct for this pejorative use: the images have no more substance than wind. The three parallel nothings (emptiness, comes to nothing, wind/futility) create a total verdict of non-existence for the idol system.
